mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the propositional calculus, predicate calculus, modal logic, necessity, and decision theory central to sections A and C.
Directly addresses completeness, consistency, decidability, and undecidability of formal mathematical systems (B.3), including Hilbert's programme.
Set theory, formal systems, and mathematical abstraction underpin set theory and natural-number arithmetic (A.5) and the formal language concepts in B.
Russell's theory of types, Principia Mathematica, and analysis are foundational to the formal logical systems in sections A and B.
Wittgenstein's Tractatus contributed to the propositional calculus and the philosophy of formal languages discussed in A.1 and B.
Analytic philosophy is rooted in Frege's and Russell's formal logic, deeply connected to model theory and metalogical studies in this section.
Aristotle developed syllogistic theory (A.3), the foundational treatment of the syllogism in formal logic.
Leibniz's characteristica universalis and logical calculus are precursors to the formal propositional and predicate calculi (A.1, A.2).
Directly relevant to the theory of argumentation and the new rhetoric (C.1.a) in applied logic.
Directly relevant to epistemic logic (C.2) dealing with the formal logic of belief, knowledge, assertion, and doubt.
Covers deduction and reasoning relevant to the critique of forms of reasoning and analysis of logical fallacies (C.1).

mappings.vsiAuthor: Emma Borg and Sarah A. Fisher
mappings.rationaleAI: Formal semantics and speech acts connect to logical semantics and model theory (B.5) and the applied logic of language.
Information theory concepts including communication and probability relate to epistemic logic (C.2) and the foundations in B.
Algorithms and Turing machines relate directly to computability, decidability, and the metalogical discoveries in B.3.
AI builds on formal logic and automata theory; Turing and von Neumann's work connects to practical logic and computational applications (C.3, C.4).
Set theory and natural-number arithmetic (A.5) connect to number-theoretic foundations used in metalogical proofs.
Covers proof, rigour, and convergence relevant to the formal methods underlying logical calculi and syntax of formal systems (A, B).
Set theory and natural-number arithmetic (A.5) involve infinite sets and cardinality, central to the study of infinity.
Expected utility and decision-making relate to practical logic dealing with concepts of choosing and planning (C.3).
Combinatorics involves formal mathematical reasoning about discrete structures, relating to the formal systems discussed in A and B.
